title: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Persistent Chemotherapy-Induced Alopecia Among Women With Breast Cancer.
gap_statement: Existing studies on persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) have been conducted within racially and ethnically homogeneous populations, limiting generalizability, and PCIA-related psychological distress has rarely been compared across racial and ethnic groups using harmonized measures.
framing_pattern: Multiracial/multiethnic evidence gap + harmonization gap: 'existing studies on PCIA have been conducted within racially and ethnically homogeneous populations, limiting generalizability... the psychological distress associated with PCIA has rarely been compared across racial and ethnic groups using harmonized measures' (Introduction).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Bi-national prospective cohort combining the CHANCE study at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (US, 2015-2021) and the CHANCE Korea twin cohort at Samsung Medical Center (2018-2022), with standardized trichoscopy and the validated CADS distress scale.
methodology_choice.rationale: Harmonized protocols, eligibility criteria, trichoscopy equipment, and CADS measures across the two sites were used to ensure comparability across racial and ethnic groups.

key_finding: Asian women had the highest PCIA incidence (42.1%) versus White (22.2%), Black (10.0%), and Hispanic/Latino (5.1%) women (adjusted risk ratio for Asian vs White 2.29, 95% CI 1.07-4.88), and also showed the greatest increase in CADS-measured distress, especially in emotional and activity domains.
limitations: Multi-country enrollment may introduce measurement variability despite harmonization; participation may have selected appearance-conscious patients; small Black and Hispanic/Latino samples limited precision; psychosocial covariates (education, employment, mental health history) were incompletely collected; findings may not generalize to newer agents such as antibody-drug conjugates; observational design precludes causal inference.
future_work: Large-scale, multiethnic prospective cohorts with harmonized clinical, genetic, and psychosocial data; prospective characterization of ADC-related alopecia; evaluation of pharmacogenetic screening (e.g., rs1202179, CYP3A4, CYP2D6) for risk stratification; culturally tailored preventive interventions.

title: Autologous vs. implant-based breast reconstruction and long-term mental disorder risk: a retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial.
gap_statement: Prior studies comparing autologous (AR) and implant-based (IBR) breast reconstruction focused largely on short-term aesthetic or psychological outcomes within 12 months, rarely examined clinically diagnosed mental disorders as long-term outcomes, lacked rigorous control for baseline mental health, and were limited to single-center or small cohorts, leaving the long-term psychological impact poorly understood.
framing_pattern: Inconsistency across prior studies + structural limitations: 'The inconsistency across prior studies likely stems from several key limitations. First, many have focused primarily on short-term outcomes... Second, few have examined clinically diagnosed mental disorders as long-term outcomes. Third, prior analyses often lacked rigorous control for baseline mental health conditions. Lastly, existing literature tends to be limited to single-center or small cohort studies' (Introduction).
hypothesis: AR would be associated with a higher long-term risk of clinically diagnosed mental disorders compared with IBR at 1, 5, and up to 9 years after reconstruction.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) nationwide database 2015-2023, with 1:3 propensity-score matching (5113 AR vs 14 738 IBR among 24 930 eligible patients).
methodology_choice.rationale: Target trial emulation with a nationwide administrative database was chosen to address prior limitations: minimal loss to follow-up, objective claims-based mental health outcomes, and population-level generalizability beyond single institutions; an exploratory radiotherapy validation analysis served as a positive control.

key_finding: AR was associated with a higher long-term risk of any clinically diagnosed mental disorder (aHR 1.13, 95% CI 1.07-1.19), with the strongest signal in anxiety (aHR 1.25) and a more pronounced effect in patients aged >=50 years (aHR 1.16); risk persisted through 5 years, while delayed AR in younger patients showed a reversed direction (aHR 0.79, interaction p=0.021).
limitations: Observational design cannot prove causation despite target trial emulation and PS matching; psychiatric outcomes defined by administrative claims; residual confounding from unmeasured psychosocial variables (marital status, baseline psychosocial functioning, chronic pain, lymphedema); reconstruction type defined by procedure codes only; findings may not generalize beyond Korea.
future_work: Integration of mental health screening and age-sensitive, individualized counseling into preoperative decision-making; replication in other healthcare systems.

title: Quality of life, function, and symptom trajectory patterns following salivary gland cancer treatment: a 5-year prospective cohort study.
gap_statement: Longitudinal patterns of patient-reported quality of life (QoL) remain poorly understood in patients with salivary gland cancer (SGC), and baseline characteristics associated with adverse recovery patterns have not been well characterized.
framing_pattern: Long-term PRO trajectory gap in a rare cancer: 'Longitudinal patterns of patient-reported quality of life (QoL) remain poorly understood in patients with salivary gland cancer' (Abstract/Introduction).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Prospective single-center cohort at a tertiary cancer center from 2016, with 252 histologically confirmed SGC patients followed for up to 5 years using EORTC QLQ-C30 and H&N35 questionnaires; group-based trajectory analysis applied.
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: Three distinct global QoL trajectories were identified - Stable Good (52.4%), Declining (16.2%), and Delayed Recovery (31.3%) - with female sex, older age, worse baseline pain, and recurrence predicting unstable trajectories, while treatment intensity was not a significant predictor.
future_work: Trajectory-based classifications may support early identification of vulnerable survivors and inform risk-stratified supportive care targeting pain, nutrition, and emotional function.

title: Association of maternal pre-existing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and neonatal and long-term offspring outcomes: a nationwide mother-child paired cohort.
gap_statement: Little is known about the effects of maternal pre-existing ASCVD on offspring outcomes beyond the neonatal period; most existing research has focused on short-term obstetric complications or maternal outcomes, with few studies examining long-term developmental risks, and prior studies have been limited by small sample sizes and lacked linkage between maternal cardiovascular history and child health data.
framing_pattern: Long-term offspring outcome gap + data-linkage gap: 'little is known about the effects of maternal ASCVD on offspring outcomes, particularly beyond the neonatal period... prior studies have often been limited by small sample sizes and have lacked linkage between maternal cardiovascular history and child health data' (Introduction).
hypothesis: Maternal pre-existing ASCVD adversely influences both neonatal and long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes in offspring.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) mother-child paired cohort 2005-2019 with follow-up through 2020; 5 461 222 live births, 3 640 815 unique mothers, with 1:4 propensity-score matching (145 315 ASCVD vs 581 260 non-ASCVD).
methodology_choice.rationale: Nationwide mother-child linkage via unique resident registration numbers was used to address prior small-sample and unlinked-data limitations; mixed-effects models accounted for within-mother correlation of repeated births.

key_finding: Offspring of mothers with pre-existing ASCVD had increased risks of major congenital malformations (aOR 1.09), NICU admission (aOR 1.19), neonatal sepsis (aOR 1.11), and neurodevelopmental disorders (aHR 1.08); risks persisted irrespective of adverse pregnancy outcomes, suggesting a direct contribution of ASCVD beyond APO-mediated pathways.
limitations: Retrospective administrative data may introduce information bias; only registered live births included so terminations/fetal loss not captured; ASCVD identified by ICD-10 codes without clinical chart verification; lacked detailed information on ASCVD medications and adherence during pregnancy; confined to Korea, limiting racial generalizability.
future_work: Replication across nations with diverse racial demographics; ethically designed prospective and interventional studies on cardiovascular medications during pregnancy; mechanistic studies on vascular, inflammatory, and pharmacologic pathways linking maternal ASCVD to offspring outcomes.

title: Statins in Type 1 Diabetes: Bridging the Evidence Gap With Target Trial Emulation.
gap_statement: Randomized trial evidence on statins for primary cardiovascular prevention in type 1 diabetes is lacking because T1D patients have been underrepresented in landmark statin trials, creating an evidence gap that current guidelines must navigate.
framing_pattern: Underrepresentation in RCT evidence + appeal for causal real-world inference: 'Bridging the Evidence Gap With Target Trial Emulation' (title); editorial framing of T1D as an RCT-evidence-gap population that warrants target trial emulation using real-world data (Editorial commentary).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: JACC editorial commentary on a target trial emulation study of statins in type 1 diabetes; no primary data source.
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: Target trial emulation using real-world data can substitute for absent RCTs in populations such as T1D where trials are infeasible, supporting causal inference about statin benefit for primary cardiovascular prevention.
future_work: Broader application of target trial emulation to evidence-gap populations underrepresented in RCTs; standardized causal-inference frameworks for real-world cardiovascular data.

title: Real-World Evidence on Low-Dose Olanzapine (<=1.25 mg) for Personalized Antipsychotic Dosing.
gap_statement: Despite the absence of formal recommendations for olanzapine doses below 5 mg and growing real-world use of very low doses (<=1.25 mg/day) particularly in vulnerable and East Asian populations, prescription patterns, clinical acceptability, and metabolic safety of low-dose olanzapine have not been systematically evaluated.
framing_pattern: Guideline-practice gap + Asian pharmacogenetic specificity: 'Despite the absence of formal recommendations for doses below 5 mg, a growing interest in low-dose olanzapine... Notably, East Asian populations have been shown to require different antipsychotic dosing strategies compared to western populations' (Introduction).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Clinical Data Warehouse (CDW), 2002-2023, with 9565 Korean patients prescribed olanzapine; low-dose (<=1.25 mg/day) vs regular-dose comparison using time-varying exposure and age-as-timescale Cox models.
methodology_choice.rationale: Real-world CDW data were used because dose-response heterogeneity and very-low-dose use are not captured by formal guidelines or RCTs, and personalized prescribing requires empirical evidence reflecting actual practice.

key_finding: 17% of olanzapine users received low-dose (<=1.25 mg), with 95.5% using gradual tapering/escalation; low-dose use was associated with significantly lower risks of diabetes (HR 0.32, 95% CI 0.17-0.62) and dyslipidemia (HR 0.59, 95% CI 0.42-0.82), and numerically lower cardiovascular and cerebrovascular event risk.
limitations: Retrospective single-center design cannot establish causation; residual confounding possible; symptom severity, relapse, and functional improvement not systematically measured; long-term outcomes limited by small number of patients on prolonged low-dose therapy; single-center setting limits non-Asian generalizability.
future_work: Patient-reported outcomes and clinical assessments for therapeutic efficacy; larger cohorts to evaluate exposure-response over time; pharmacokinetic/pharmacogenetic studies on individualized dosing; development of dedicated 1.25 mg tablet formulations outside Japan.

title: Breast Cancer Information Grand Round for Survivorship (BIG-S) prospective cohort.
gap_statement: Few existing cohorts comprehensively integrate patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and real-world healthcare utilization data, particularly in Asian populations, and a lack of an Asian breast cancer registry comparable to those in Western settings exists despite distinct demographic (~50% premenopausal in Asia vs 15-30% in the West) and tumor-microenvironment differences.
framing_pattern: Asian-specific registry gap + PRO/EMR integration gap: 'few studies comprehensively integrate patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and real-world healthcare utilization data, particularly in Asian populations... a lack of an Asian breast cancer registry comparable to that available for Western patients exists' (Background).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: BIG-S prospective single-center cohort at Samsung Medical Center, recruiting newly diagnosed breast cancer patients from November 2018 (N=2,749); integrates EMR clinical data, bioelectrical impedance body composition, ICHOM-recommended PROs, and healthcare utilization records.
methodology_choice.rationale: The cohort was designed following ICHOM PRO recommendations to uniquely link clinical data, PROs, and healthcare utilization in an Asian survivorship setting that has been underrepresented in existing Western registries (Kaiser Permanente, Sporadic and Hereditary Breast Cancer, Harvard, CANTO).

key_finding: Among 2,749 participants (mean age 49.7), mean QoL improved from 55.6 at diagnosis to 68.2 at 4 years, but fatigue and insomnia persisted, sexual functioning and future perspectives declined, and healthcare utilization shifted from plastic surgery/rehabilitation early on to gynecology, family medicine, and psychiatry after 2 years - a pattern suggesting delayed recognition of psychosocial and sexual health needs.
future_work: Use of BIG-S to guide targeted interventions and tailored survivorship-care strategies, especially for Asian breast cancer survivors; integration into early identification and management of persistent symptoms (fatigue, insomnia, sexual dysfunction).

title: Scalp cooling for preventing persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia in anthracycline-treated patients: A single-arm trial.
gap_statement: Evidence on the efficacy of scalp cooling specifically for preventing persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients is limited, as most prior scalp-cooling evidence concerns acute alopecia rather than persistent hair loss and excludes anthracycline regimens.
framing_pattern: Regimen-specific preventive evidence gap: scalp cooling for PCIA in anthracycline-treated patients framed as an underexplored preventive intervention (Title/Keywords).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Single-arm prospective trial of scalp cooling in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients (Korea); no PMC full text available beyond title/keywords.
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: Scalp cooling was evaluated as a preventive intervention for PCIA in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients within a single-arm trial design (specific quantitative results not available from abstract).
future_work: not stated

title: Clinical significance of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol percentage reduction and attained levels after percutaneous coronary intervention.
gap_statement: American and European guidelines disagree on post-PCI LDL-C management (US threshold-for-intensification >=1.8 mmol/L vs European target <1.4 mmol/L), and it remains unclear whether patients who achieve >=50% LDL-C reduction with high-intensity statins but land in the 1.4 to <1.8 mmol/L zone benefit from additional lipid-lowering therapy.
framing_pattern: Inconsistency between US and European guidelines: 'discrepancies exist between the American and European guidelines regarding thresholds for treatment intensification... versus treatment goals... This difference often causes confusion for clinicians as to whether the additional intervention is necessary' (Introduction).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) nationwide cohort 2010-2020 linked to biennial national health screenings; 135,877 adults with pre- and post-PCI LDL-C and 7.4-year median follow-up.
methodology_choice.rationale: K-NHIS coverage of nearly the entire Korean adult population with linked biennial health-screening LDL-C measurements enabled long-term real-world evaluation of percentage reduction and attained LDL-C thresholds that RCTs cannot easily address.

key_finding: Achieving >=50% LDL-C reduction was associated with lower MACCE risk (aHR 0.78) regardless of baseline LDL-C; among those achieving >=50% reduction, attained LDL-C of 1.4-<1.8 mmol/L (aHR 1.07) and >=1.8 mmol/L (aHR 1.12) had increased MACCE versus <1.4 mmol/L, supporting the more aggressive European target.
limitations: Retrospective design with unmeasured confounding (E-value 1.92); low use of ezetimibe/PCSK9 inhibitors due to long accrual period; dichotomized 50% reduction may oversimplify the continuous relationship; selection bias from requirement of second health screening within 3 years post-PCI.
future_work: Identification of most appropriate and effective strategies to achieve <1.4 mmol/L LDL-C after PCI; shared decision-making for the 1.4-<1.8 mmol/L group given marginal hard-outcome differences and cost of nonstatin add-ons.

title: Less harmful alternative for smoking: addressing concerns on the observational study of electronic cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes.
gap_statement: The widespread framing of e-cigarettes as a 'less harmful alternative' to combustible smoking is challenged by observational evidence of cardiovascular harm, but methodological concerns about that evidence (confounding, exposure definition, dual use) need to be addressed before causal claims can stand.
framing_pattern: Contradiction to the 'less harmful alternative' assumption: title explicitly frames the editorial as addressing concerns about an observational e-cigarette/CV-outcomes study, contesting the harm-reduction narrative (Editorial/Comment).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: European Heart Journal editorial/comment on an observational study of electronic cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes; no primary data.
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: The editorial argues that observational evidence on e-cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes should not be dismissed on methodological grounds alone, and that the 'less harmful alternative' framing requires scrutiny against accumulating real-world cardiovascular signals.
future_work: Improved observational designs (clearer exposure definitions, handling of dual use, causal-inference frameworks) and long-term cohorts to clarify cardiovascular risk of e-cigarettes.

title: Risk of Cancer in Newborns With Congenital Heart Disease and Their Mothers: A Nationwide Cohort Study.
gap_statement: Whether newborns with congenital heart disease (CHD) and their mothers have an elevated risk of cancer has not been established at a population level, despite biologic plausibility from shared developmental and possibly genetic pathways.
framing_pattern: Population-scale evidence gap in a rare comorbidity intersection: framed as a nationwide cohort letter examining whether CHD newborns and their mothers share an elevated cancer risk (Letter/Title).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Circulation research letter reporting a Korean nationwide cohort study (likely K-NHIS) of newborns with congenital heart disease and their mothers; no PMC full text and no abstract available, so detail is limited to title.
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: Reports population-level cancer risk among newborns with congenital heart disease and their mothers using nationwide cohort data (specific risk estimates not available from abstract).
future_work: not stated

title: Development and Validation of Facial Line Distress Scale-Glabellar Lines (FINE-GL).
gap_statement: Existing aesthetic-outcome measures for glabellar lines (photographic scales like the Facial Wrinkle Scale, Glabellar Line Scale, and Merz Aesthetic Scale) are single-item, severity-only tools that do not capture psychosocial distress, and existing facial-line PROMs (Facial Line Satisfaction Questionnaire, Facial Line Outcomes, FACE-Q) are designed for general facial lines and do not adequately capture concerns specific to the glabellar area.
framing_pattern: Instrument gap + condition-specificity gap: 'they are based on a single question and only assess the severity of lines without assessing their consequent psychological distress... existing measures are designed for general facial lines, and they may not adequately capture the unique functional and aesthetic concerns specific to the glabellar area' (Introduction).
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Two-phase instrument development per COSMIN and US FDA PROM guidance: qualitative interviews (n=25) and cognitive interviews (n=15) for content validation, followed by a cross-sectional survey at one tertiary hospital and two local clinics in Korea (n=198) with EFA, IRT-GRM, CFA, and test-retest analyses.
methodology_choice.rationale: COSMIN and FDA PROM-development frameworks were followed to ensure rigorous content, construct, and criterion validity for a condition-specific instrument.

key_finding: FINE-GL is a 20-item, 2-domain (appearance appraisal; distress due to GL) condition-specific PROM with good model fit (CFI 0.918, SRMR 0.05), Cronbach's alpha 0.92-0.97, test-retest ICC 0.77-0.90, and AUC 0.73 for predicting physician-rated severe GL (cut-off 50.6).
limitations: No existing GL-specific questionnaire was available for convergent-validity comparison; cross-sectional design precluded longitudinal validity/responsiveness assessment; recruitment from only two Korean local hospitals plus one tertiary site limits generalizability.
future_work: Longitudinal studies to confirm responsiveness of FINE-GL to clinical improvement or deterioration; broader multi-site validation.

title: Effects of proton pump inhibitors on gastrointestinal bleeding and cardiovascular outcomes in myocardial infarction patients treated with DAPT.
gap_statement: A discrepancy exists between European and American guideline recommendations for routine use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) in patients on dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT), leaving clinicians without clear evidence on whether PPI co-prescription improves the bleeding-ischaemia trade-off after AMI in East Asian populations.
framing_pattern: International guideline inconsistency + missing real-world East Asian evidence; framed as a need to 'determine the association between the co-prescription of PPIs and DAPT and the occurrence of gastrointestinal bleeding and ischaemic events' in nationwide AMI/PCI patients.
hypothesis: Co-prescription of PPIs with DAPT after PCI for AMI is associated with reduced major gastrointestinal bleeding without increasing major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (MACCE).

methodology_choice.data_source: Nationwide Korean claims database of AMI patients undergoing PCI with DAPT (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06241833)
methodology_choice.rationale: Large-scale propensity score matching on nationwide claims was used to emulate guideline-relevant comparisons in real-world East Asian patients where randomized evidence is lacking.

key_finding: After propensity-score matching of 35,560 pairs, PPI co-prescription was associated with significantly lower 1-year major gastrointestinal bleeding (0.7% vs 0.4%, HR 0.59, 95% CI 0.48-0.73) without a difference in MACCE (HR 0.98, 95% CI 0.94-1.02), consistent across P2Y inhibitor type, PPI type, and bleeding-risk strata.
future_work: not stated

title: Combustible cigarette smoking harms nearly every organ in the body.
gap_statement: Public and clinical discourse continues to underestimate the multi-organ harms of combustible cigarette smoking, motivating an editorial framing that smoking is not only a cardiovascular or pulmonary issue but a whole-body health threat.
framing_pattern: Editorial framing of smoking harm as systemic ('nearly every organ in the body'); positions combustible cigarettes as the reference baseline against which alternative nicotine products must be judged.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: European Heart Journal editorial commentary (no primary data)
methodology_choice.rationale: Editorial format used to contextualize accompanying empirical work on smoking and cardiovascular outcomes for the journal's readership.

key_finding: Combustible cigarette smoking is reaffirmed as harmful to nearly every organ system, and clinicians and policy makers should treat it as a systemic disease driver rather than an organ-specific risk factor.
future_work: not stated

title: Prognosis after switching to electronic cigarettes following percutaneous coronary intervention: a Korean nationwide study.
gap_statement: Despite the increasing popularity of electronic cigarettes, the prognostic impact of switching to e-cigarettes among smokers with coronary artery disease after percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) remains unclear, leaving clinicians without evidence to counsel post-PCI smokers about harm-reduction options.
framing_pattern: Knowledge gap on real-world prognosis of post-PCI behaviour change; framed as 'the prognostic impact of switching to E-cigarettes ... remains unclear' and resolved using a nationwide post-PCI smoking-trajectory cohort.
hypothesis: Among smokers who undergo PCI, switching to e-cigarettes (especially exclusive use) is associated with lower major adverse cardiac events (MACE) than continued combustible cigarette use, with risk closer to that of successful quitters.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance nationwide cohort of 17,973 post-PCI smokers with pre- and post-PCI health screening (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06338761)
methodology_choice.rationale: Nationwide linked claims and health-screening data allowed classification of post-PCI smoking trajectories (continued, switched to e-cig, dual user, quitter) at population scale, which is infeasible in trials.

key_finding: Over a median 2.4-year follow-up, switchers to e-cigarettes (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.69-0.98) and successful quitters (HR 0.87, 95% CI 0.79-0.96) had lower MACE risk than continued combustible smokers, and exclusive e-cigarette use was associated with lower MACE than dual use (HR 0.71, 95% CI 0.51-0.99).
future_work: not stated

title: Measuring patient-reported distress from breast magnetic resonance imaging: Development and validation of the MRI-related distress scale (MRI-DS).
gap_statement: Although breast MRI is a valuable screening tool, MRI testing burden is associated with cancer worry and reduced quality of life, yet existing PROMs such as STAI, HADS, and the Testing Morbidity Index are designed for general anxiety or generic HRQoL and 'are inadequate for assessing the MRI-related anxiety,' leaving no instrument able to capture comprehensive, breast-MRI-specific distress.
framing_pattern: Absence of a fit-for-purpose PRO instrument; framed as 'the absence of a suitable PRO measure (PROM) for evaluating patient-centered issues related to MRI,' addressed by following the COSMIN checklist to develop and validate a breast-MRI-specific scale.
hypothesis: A breast-MRI-specific PROM (MRI-DS) can be developed and validated to capture physical, psychological, practical, and injection-related distress that is distinct from general anxiety or HRQoL.

methodology_choice.data_source: Two-phase study at a Korean tertiary university hospital: Phase I cognitive interviews (n=8) and Phase II psychometric validation (n=180) at Samsung Medical Center Breast Cancer Center outpatient clinic (Apr-Aug 2023)
methodology_choice.rationale: COSMIN-guided two-phase design (item development with qualitative interviews followed by quantitative EFA/CFA and known-group analysis) was chosen to ensure both content and construct validity for an MRI-specific PROM.

key_finding: The 18-item, four-factor MRI-DS (physical, psychological, practical, injection-related distress) showed good factor structure, internal consistency (Cronbach alpha 0.82-0.91, total 0.91), discriminant validity (weak correlation with STAI and WHOQOL-BREF, moderate with QOL-CS-K fear-of-future-testing items), and known-group validity (higher scores in patients who rated MRI as the most burdensome breast examination); 97.2% of patients reported at least one troublesome MRI symptom.
limitations: Single-hospital convenience sample with few patients followed without breast cancer; no test-retest analysis was performed; convergent validity of physical, practical, and injection-related domains was not tested against separate instruments to limit respondent burden; CFI value was modest (0.881) because items capturing unique unmet needs were retained even when weakly correlated.
future_work: A future validation study should assess the MRI-DS in non-breast-cancer populations requiring breast MRI, and the scale should be applied to compare patient-reported impact of abbreviated MRI (AB-MRI) versus full dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI for shared decision-making.

title: Scalp Cooling in Preventing Persistent Chemotherapy-Induced Alopecia: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
gap_statement: Existing studies of scalp cooling efficacy are limited by short-term duration and have not evaluated whether scalp cooling prevents persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) months after chemotherapy ends.
framing_pattern: Methodological limitation of prior evidence ('current studies of the efficacy of scalp cooling are limited by short-term duration'), addressed by extending follow-up to 6 months post-chemotherapy with objective hair-thickness/density measurement.
hypothesis: Scalp cooling during chemotherapy reduces the incidence and severity of persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia at 6 months after chemotherapy completion compared with usual care.

methodology_choice.data_source: Open-label 2:1 randomized controlled trial in newly diagnosed stage I-III breast cancer patients receiving neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy (Dec 2020-Aug 2021), with hair thickness and density measured by Folliscope 5.0 and CIA-related distress measured with CADS
methodology_choice.rationale: An RCT with objective Folliscope-based hair thickness/density assessment and validated CADS distress measurement was chosen to overcome the short-term, subjective limitations of prior scalp-cooling studies.

key_finding: At 6 months post-chemotherapy, PCIA occurred in 13.5% (12/89) of the scalp-cooling group versus 52.0% (26/50) of controls; scalp cooling increased hair thickness (mean difference 9.0 microns) and reduced CIA-related distress (CADS difference -3.2), with a small density difference (-3.3 hairs/cm).
future_work: Further research is necessary to observe longer-term benefits of scalp cooling beyond 6 months.

title: Emulating trial to evaluate the effectiveness of routine supportive care on mortality among cancer patients experiencing distress at the time of diagnosis.
gap_statement: Few studies have evaluated the effectiveness of interventions for distress during cancer diagnosis on clinical outcomes such as mortality in a real-world setting, leaving the value of routine information and psychosocial support for distressed newly diagnosed patients unproven.
framing_pattern: Real-world evidence gap on whether psychosocial care affects hard outcomes; framed as the need to test whether 'routine information and psychosocial support to patients experiencing distress at the time of diagnosis could decrease the risk of mortality,' addressed by emulating a target trial in routinely collected screening data.
hypothesis: Among newly diagnosed cancer patients with distress scores >=4 at screening, receipt of routine information and psychosocial support reduces 1-year and 3-year all-cause mortality compared with no such support.

methodology_choice.data_source: Retrospective cohort of 4,880 newly diagnosed cancer patients screened with tablet/kiosk-based distress assessment at a university-affiliated cancer center in Seoul, South Korea (Jul 2014-Dec 2017)
methodology_choice.rationale: A target-trial emulation with two groups (received vs did not receive supportive care) and Cox models was used to estimate causal effects on mortality in a real-world clinical setting where randomized trials of routine distress care are difficult.

key_finding: Receipt of routine information and psychosocial support was associated with a 27% lower 1-year mortality (HR 0.73, 95% CI 0.54-0.99), with a stronger effect in patients aged <60 (p for interaction 0.03), while the 3-year mortality reduction was non-significant (HR 0.93, 95% CI 0.76-1.14).
future_work: not stated

title: Comprehensive Young Age Breast Cancer registry from clinical, genomics, and patient-reported outcomes measured with 15 years follow-up: the CHARM cohort profile.
gap_statement: Young breast cancer (YBC) patients face distinct clinical challenges and research gaps that are inadequately captured by existing breast cancer registries, which typically lack integrated clinical, multi-omics, fertility-related, and patient-reported outcome data with long-term follow-up.
framing_pattern: Population-specific data gap; framed as 'distinct clinical challenges and research gaps in young breast cancer (YBC) patients,' addressed by establishing a dedicated prospective registry that integrates clinical records, multi-omics tissue/blood biobanking, reproductive hormones, and mobile PROs.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Comprehensive Young Age Breast Cancer (CHARM) registry at Samsung Medical Center (since April 2013); patients <=40 at diagnosis or pregnant/postpartum within 1 year, with electronic medical record data, Medidata Rave EDC, central pathology review, annual bone density, multi-omics tumor/blood samples, reproductive hormone panels (AMH, estradiol, FSH, LH), and mobile PRO questionnaires
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective cohort with integrated clinical, multi-omics, fertility, and PRO data was designed to address the lack of population-specific evidence and to enable 15-year follow-up tailored to YBC research priorities.

key_finding: The SMC YBC cohort enrolled 1,868 participants (mean age 35.57 years, 99.8% premenopausal), of whom 1,062 completed PRO questionnaires, establishing a comprehensive YBC platform to optimize care and knowledge for this underserved population.
future_work: not stated

title: Impact of Social Support during Diagnosis and Treatment on Disease Progression in Young Patients with Breast Cancer: A Prospective Cohort Study.
gap_statement: Although social isolation has been linked to mortality and recurrence in mixed-age cancer cohorts, the impact of changes in social support after diagnosis on clinical outcomes among young breast cancer patients (<=40 years) is not well understood, even though family and work demands make social support especially critical at this life stage.
framing_pattern: Population- and exposure-specific evidence gap; framed as 'the impact of social support decline after treatment on clinical outcomes among young patients (<= 40 years old) with breast cancer is not well understood,' addressed by examining change (not just baseline level) of social support in a young breast cancer registry.
hypothesis: Among young breast cancer patients, a decrease in social/family well-being (SFWB) between diagnosis and active treatment is associated with worse recurrence-free survival (RFS) compared with patients whose SFWB increases.

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Young Breast Cancer Registry (NCT03131089), 2013-2021; 610 patients <=40 years with FACT-G Social/Family Well-Being measured before and during treatment
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective cohort with repeated FACT-G measurements allowed modelling of change in SFWB and use of Cox regression with inverse probability censoring weights to estimate the association with RFS while controlling for clinical and sociodemographic confounders.

key_finding: Between diagnosis and during treatment, 53.8% of young breast cancer patients had decreasing SFWB; the decreasing group had a 2.49-fold higher risk of recurrence or death versus the increasing group (adjusted HR 2.49, 95% CI 1.04-5.98), with decreasing emotional family support showing the strongest association (HR 4.03, 95% CI 1.16-13.96); having children at diagnosis was the main risk factor for decreasing SFWB.
limitations: Most data were self-reported, exact biological mechanisms linking decreased SFWB to poor RFS could not be established, residual confounding from the observational design remains, distressed patients may have refused participation (underestimating change), and the cohort was Asian and well educated, limiting generalizability.
future_work: Further biological studies are required to confirm mechanisms (e.g., inflammation, oxytocin pathways), and health professionals should develop family-based psychosocial interventions including children and fathers to support young breast cancer patients.

title: Pretreatment endocrine symptoms and recurrence-free survival among young premenopausal patients with breast cancer: a prospective cohort study.
gap_statement: Although pretreatment vasomotor symptoms have been linked to breast cancer mortality in postmenopausal women, only a few studies have evaluated the association between pretreatment endocrine symptoms and clinical outcomes in premenopausal patients with breast cancer, in part because prior population-based cohorts contained too few young premenopausal cases to assess this question.
framing_pattern: Age- and menopausal-status-specific evidence gap; framed as 'only a few studies have evaluated the association between pretreatment endocrine symptoms and clinical outcomes among premenopausal patients with breast cancer,' addressed by leveraging a large young breast cancer registry with systematic FACT-ES at diagnosis.
hypothesis: Higher pretreatment endocrine symptom burden (measured by FACT-ES) is associated with worse recurrence-free survival in premenopausal breast cancer patients aged <=40 years.

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Young Breast Cancer Registry (NCT03131089), 2013-2021; 977 premenopausal patients <=40 years with FACT-ES at diagnosis
methodology_choice.rationale: A large prospective young breast cancer cohort with standardized FACT-ES at diagnosis was used to overcome the small premenopausal sample sizes of prior studies, and Cox/Fine-Gray models adjusted for clinical and quality-of-life confounders to test endocrine symptoms as a prognostic marker.

key_finding: 94.7% of young premenopausal breast cancer patients reported at least one pretreatment endocrine symptom and 17.2% had at least one severe symptom; the high-symptom group had a 2.05-fold higher risk of recurrence or death than the low-symptom group (adjusted HR 2.05, 95% CI 1.19-3.54), with severe hot flashes (HR 5.63) and breast sensitivity/tenderness (HR 1.82) being key drivers, and symptom score correlated with the systemic immune-inflammation index.
limitations: Underlying mechanisms could not be established because hormone measurements were not available at diagnosis, residual confounding from the observational design is possible, and reverse causation cannot be excluded (some symptoms might reflect undiagnosed cancer), although the majority of participants had stage I/II disease detected by screening.
future_work: A multidisciplinary approach and further biological studies are needed to identify mechanisms linking pretreatment endocrine symptoms with prognosis and to establish optimal management strategies for young breast cancer patients.

title: Prediction Model for Postoperative Quality of Life Among Breast Cancer Survivors Along the Survivorship Trajectory From Pretreatment to 5 Years: Machine Learning-Based Analysis.
gap_statement: Existing prediction models for quality of life (QoL) in breast cancer survivors are limited because they (1) include only a few dimensions and miss key variables such as spiritual well-being, (2) rarely cover long-term survivors, (3) ignore the time-varying nature of QoL-related factors, and (4) lack interpretability and external validation, leaving clinicians without tools to tailor survivorship care across the trajectory.
framing_pattern: Multi-pronged methodological gap explicitly enumerated as four 'First/Second/Third/Fourth' limitations of prior ML models; framed as 'traditional statistical models have limitations in identifying factors associated with QoL over time,' addressed with an interpretable ML pipeline (SHAP) trained per survival phase with external validation.
hypothesis: An interpretable machine-learning pipeline using SHAP-based feature selection can predict poor QoL across different survivorship phases (pretreatment to 5 years) and identify time-varying patient-centered predictors that generalize to an external cohort.

methodology_choice.data_source: Development set: Breast Cancer Information Grand Round for Survivorship (BIG-S) cross-sectional study at Samsung Medical Center (2018-2019, n=6,265); external validation set: Beauty Education for Distressed Breast Cancer (BEST) longitudinal cohort at two Seoul university-based hospitals (2011-2016, n=432)
methodology_choice.rationale: Six ML algorithms (DNN, GBM, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, random forest) with SHAP-based recursive feature elimination, 10-fold cross-validation, and external validation on an independent cohort were chosen to overcome interpretability and generalizability limitations of prior QoL prediction models.

key_finding: The ML models achieved good performance across survivorship phases (AUC 0.808-0.860 in training; 0.770-0.862 in external validation); emotional function was most important pretreatment, physical function within 1 year post-surgery, fatigue between 1-4 years, and hopefulness was the single most influential feature across all phases.
limitations: Cross-sectional design means causal direction between QoL and symptoms/function could be reversed; QoL was measured with a single EORTC-C30 item (though validated); results may not generalize to other cancer survivors or settings.
future_work: Further studies in other cancer survivor populations are needed to confirm generalizability, and future research should focus on developing and testing interventions for physical function, fatigue, and hope-promoting psychosocial care across the survivorship trajectory.

title: Surveillance of Symptom Burden Using the Patient-Reported Outcome Version of the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events in Patients With Various Types of Cancers During Chemoradiation Therapy: Real-World Study.
gap_statement: Existing symptom-measure guidelines for cancer were designed for clinical trials and provide little guidance for real-world surveillance; prior PRO-CTCAE subsets have focused on common Western cancers (breast, lung, colorectal) and lack coverage of less common cancers such as lymphoma, stomach, and liver cancer, and differences in symptom profiles across cancer types have not been fully elucidated for real-world surveillance.
framing_pattern: Mismatch between trial-oriented PRO guidance and real-world clinical needs; framed as 'a lack of guidance for practitioners and for performing surveillance in the real-world clinical setting' and the unmet need to characterize cancer-specific symptom profiles using cancer-tailored PRO-CTCAE subsets.
hypothesis: Symptom frequency and severity measured with cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE subsets differ by cancer type during chemotherapy or radiation therapy, and higher symptom burden is associated with poorer health-related quality of life.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional tablet-based PRO survey of 1,352 outpatients receiving chemotherapy and/or radiation therapy at the National Cancer Center (Goyang) and Samsung Medical Center (Seoul) between December 2017 and January 2018, using 10 cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE-Korean subsets developed via Delphi
methodology_choice.rationale: A two-center cross-sectional design with Delphi-derived cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE subsets and EORTC QLQ-C30 was used to capture real-world symptom heterogeneity across diverse cancer types and link PRO symptoms to HRQoL via linear regression.

key_finding: Gastrointestinal symptoms dominated across all cancer types; fatigue (76.5%), decreased appetite (65.4%), and numbness/tingling (57.5%) were the most frequent, with substantial site-specific patterns (e.g., decreased libido in >50% of colorectal, gynecologic, breast, and lung cancers; pain during sexual intercourse in 59.8% of gynecologic/lymphoma patients); worsening PRO-CTCAE scores were associated with clinically meaningful HRQoL declines (e.g., fatigue coefficient -8.15).
limitations: PRO reporting was voluntary, introducing potential participation bias (lower reporting in males and older patients); symptoms were captured only at outpatient visits, missing hospitalized or hospice patients who may be most symptomatic; treatment-modality effects were not assessed because of cohort heterogeneity; symptoms of unique cancers within broader categories were not separately described.
future_work: Holistic, multi-symptom monitoring and management strategies based on comprehensive PROs are needed in routine care, and the approach of linking routinely collected PROs to large population-based health care databases should be expanded.

title: Divorce after breast cancer diagnosis and its impact on quality of life.
gap_statement: Factors associated with divorce following breast cancer diagnosis and the consequences of such divorce for survivors' quality of life remain inadequately characterized, particularly in Asian settings, leaving clinicians without clear targets for early psychosocial intervention.
framing_pattern: Under-researched psychosocial sequela; framed as a need to 'identify factors associated with divorce following breast cancer diagnosis and measure the impact of divorce on the quality of life (QoL) of patients,' addressed through a multi-site outpatient survey of Korean survivors.
hypothesis: Sociodemographic factors (younger age, lower education, employment at diagnosis) are associated with higher likelihood of divorce after breast cancer diagnosis, and divorced survivors experience worse QoL, social functioning, and body image than those who remain married.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional survey of 4,366 adult breast cancer survivors who completed active treatment without recurrence, collected at breast cancer outpatient clinics in South Korea (November 2018 to April 2019)
methodology_choice.rationale: A multi-clinic cross-sectional survey with logistic and linear regression was used to identify risk factors for post-diagnosis divorce and to compare QoL between divorced and married survivors in a large Korean sample.

key_finding: Approximately 11.1 per 1,000 married breast cancer survivors experienced divorce after diagnosis; younger age, lower education, and being employed at diagnosis were risk factors; divorced survivors reported significantly lower overall QoL (coef -7.50), social functioning (coef -9.47), and body image, and more pain, insomnia, financial difficulties, and hair-loss-related distress.
future_work: Identifying risk factors of divorce will help ascertain the resources necessary for early psychosocial intervention in breast cancer survivors.

title: Pre-treatment quality of life in patients with salivary gland cancer in comparison with those of head and neck cancer patients.
gap_statement: Because salivary gland cancer (SGC) is rare, there are limited data on pre-treatment quality of life (QoL) in SGC patients, and how it compares to that of more common head and neck cancer (HNC) subtypes is poorly characterized.
framing_pattern: Rare-disease evidence gap framed via comparison to a reference HNC group: SGC has 'a variety of clinical and biological behaviors different from head and neck cancer' yet 'limited data on pre-treatment QoL' exist.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Prospective head and neck cancer registry (2016-2020) of 225 SGC patients and 912 oral cavity/oropharyngeal cancer controls; EORTC QLQ-C30 and H&N35 modules (NCT02546895).
methodology_choice.rationale: Use of an established prospective HNC registry allowed adequate sample size despite SGC rarity and enabled direct comparison with HNC controls diagnosed in the same period.

key_finding: SGC patients had better overall baseline QoL, emotional, cognitive, and social functioning and fewer symptoms than HNC patients, but advanced-stage SGC was associated with disproportionately lower role and emotional functioning, especially among females and younger patients (stage I vs IV difference -12.9).
future_work: not stated

title: Effectiveness of Self-Assessment, TAilored Information, and Lifestyle Management for Cancer Patients Returning to Work (START): A Multi-Center Randomized Controlled Trial.
gap_statement: Although about 40% of cancer patients who quit work do so before treatment begins, most prior return-to-work (RTW) interventions targeted post-treatment survivors and addressed only psychosocial support or symptom management, ignoring stigma, fear of recurrence, and the meaning of work that drive early job loss.
framing_pattern: 'Failed prior trials + missed timing + missed targets' framing: 'small or no effects were shown in previous RTW trials' and 'previous interventions did not tackle these issues,' so a more comprehensive intervention 'from immediately after diagnosis to after treatment' is needed.
hypothesis: An individually tailored RTW intervention combining self-assessment, tailored information, and lifestyle management delivered from diagnosis through post-treatment will increase the proportion of cancer patients who return to work compared with usual care.

methodology_choice.data_source: Multi-center two-arm RCT in Seoul and Suwon (Oct 2018-Feb 2020; CRIS KCT0003262); 239 newly diagnosed working-age cancer patients randomized 1:1 to START vs control, stratified by hospital, cancer type, and job collar.
methodology_choice.rationale: An RCT was needed to test efficacy of a complex behavioral intervention; stratified randomization controlled for cancer- and job-related heterogeneity, and the intervention timing was set at diagnosis based on prior qualitative and quantitative work showing most patients quit before treatment.

key_finding: The START group had a significantly higher RTW rate than controls at 1 month post-intervention (65.4% vs 55.9%, p=0.037), with stronger effects among patients not working at baseline, and significantly better RTW-related knowledge (7.4 vs 6.8, p=0.029); 12-month RTW was numerically but not significantly higher.
limitations: About one-fourth of intervention participants received only partial intervention; baseline imbalance in working status between arms; single-country (Korean) two-center sample limits generalizability.
future_work: Develop an online/digital RTW education program accessible irrespective of time and place; conduct larger trials focused on patients not working at baseline; test in different settings.

title: Changes in working status after cancer diagnosis and socio-demographic, clinical, work-related, and psychological factors associated with it.
gap_statement: Although many studies have described changes in working status after cancer, few comprehensively evaluate the combined socio-demographic, clinical, work-related, and psychological factors involved, and prior literature is largely limited to breast cancer survivors in Western countries where cultural specificity may not transfer.
framing_pattern: 'Broad descriptive base, narrow explanatory base' framing: while work-status changes are well described, 'few studies evaluated factors associated with changes in the working status of cancer survivors comprehensively,' and Western data may not apply because work factors have 'cultural specificity.'
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional multi-center survey of 730 working-age (20-65) cancer patients who were working at diagnosis, recruited from Samsung Medical Center (Seoul) and Chonnam National University Hospital (Oct 2017-Mar 2018); Korean Occupational Stress Scale and Work and Meaning Inventory.
methodology_choice.rationale: A cross-sectional design with validated occupational stress and meaning-of-work instruments allowed simultaneous assessment of a wide range of socio-demographic, clinical, and work-related factors in a single Korean working-age cancer cohort.

key_finding: 29% of working-age cancer survivors discontinued working, with 55% of those quitting immediately after diagnosis; being female (RRR 3.65), not being the principal wage earner (RRR 6.76), blue-collar work, advanced stage, recurrence, high occupational stress (especially lack of reward, RRR 11.76), and low meaning of work were independently associated with work cessation.
limitations: Self-reported working-status changes subject to social-desirability and recall bias; two-center Korean sample limits generalizability; need for prospective follow-up.
future_work: Conduct prospective longitudinal studies to confirm findings; design public campaigns to reduce workplace cancer stigma; develop occupational-healthcare systems and multidisciplinary RTW programs that combine occupational counseling with supervised physical exercise.

title: Temporal patterns of chronic disease incidence after breast cancer: a nationwide population-based cohort study.
gap_statement: Prior studies have not provided detailed descriptions of the temporal pattern of chronic disease incidence after breast cancer due to limited sample sizes, short follow-up, and lack of information on pre-cancer risk factor levels, making it hard to separate the specific contribution of breast cancer and its treatments from baseline risk.
framing_pattern: 'Limited size + limited follow-up + no baseline risk-factor data' framing: 'prior studies were usually limited by a lack of information on risk factor levels prior to the development of breast cancer, making it difficult to separate the specific contribution of breast cancer or breast cancer-related therapies to the development of chronic disease.'
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Nationwide retrospective cohort using Korean National Health Insurance Service (NHIS) claims and biennial health screening exam data (2002-2016): 84,969 incident breast cancer cases vs 1,057,674 age- and region-matched cancer-free controls; breast cancer treated as a time-varying exposure.
methodology_choice.rationale: NHIS provides near-complete population follow-up with linkage to objective baseline risk factors (BMI, smoking, drinking, physical activity, income) from screening exams, overcoming the sample-size and confounding limitations of prior work.

key_finding: Breast cancer was associated with increased risk of leukemia (HR 4.20), cardiomyopathy (HR 3.28), osteoporosis (HR 3.00), endometrial cancer (HR 2.70), and hypothyroidism, with distinct temporal patterns: some risks (cardiomyopathy, osteoporosis, hypothyroidism) peaked early and decayed while others (leukemia, endometrial cancer) increased over follow-up; risks for leukemia, cardiomyopathy, endometrial cancer, hypothyroidism, pulmonary fibrosis, and hyperlipidemia remained elevated beyond 5 years.
limitations: No data on cancer stage and only limited treatment information from claims; potential surveillance bias because cancer patients have more contact with the health-care system; treatment modalities frequently overlapped so independent contributions could not be estimated.
future_work: Define optimal strategies for prevention, early detection, and management of chronic conditions after breast cancer; incorporate surveillance and prevention into survivorship care; further study the impact of chemotherapy and hormone therapy on thyroid disorders.

title: Psychometric Validation of the Korean Version of the Cancer Survivors' Unmet Needs (CaSUN) Scale among Korean Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Survivors.
gap_statement: The CaSUN has not been validated in lung cancer survivors or in a Korean sample; prior unmet-needs research in lung cancer used mostly qualitative methods or small samples drawn from broader multi-cancer studies, leaving long-term NSCLC survivors after curative treatment poorly characterized.
framing_pattern: 'Right tool, wrong population' framing: CaSUN is 'one of the major measurement tools' widely used in breast, colon, and prostate cancer, but 'to date, the CaSUN has not been validated in lung cancer survivors,' nor in a Korean sample, leaving NSCLC survivors' unmet needs unmeasured with a validated instrument.
hypothesis: The Korean CaSUN (CaSUN-K) will demonstrate adequate reliability and construct validity in Korean NSCLC survivors, with unmet-needs scores negatively correlated with EORTC QLQ-C30 functioning and positively correlated with fear of cancer recurrence and symptoms.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional validation study of 949 Korean NSCLC survivors (post-curative pulmonary resection, treatment ended >=1 month prior) recruited at Samsung Medical Center (Jan-Oct 2020); CaSUN-K, EORTC QLQ-C30, and K-FCRI-SF; EFA, CFA, Cronbach's alpha, convergent and known-group validity.
methodology_choice.rationale: A standard psychometric validation pipeline (forward/back-translation, expert review, cognitive interviews, EFA/CFA, convergent and known-group validity) was needed to establish cross-cultural and disease-specific applicability.

key_finding: CaSUN-K demonstrated good reliability (Cronbach's alpha 0.96 overall, 0.68-0.95 per dimension) and a six-factor structure that added a novel 'financial issues' dimension (including hospital parking) absent from the original five-factor Australian model; concern about cancer recurrence was the most frequently reported unmet need (77%), and scores discriminated by age, sex, marital status, income, and time since treatment.
limitations: Single-institution Korean sample limits generalizability; no alternative unmet-needs instrument was used to confirm convergent validity; no test-retest reliability analysis.
future_work: Develop additional CaSUN-K items measuring relationships with family members, friends, and co-workers; use CaSUN-K to design timely, targeted supportive interventions for Korean NSCLC survivors.

title: Impact of objective financial burden and subjective financial distress on spiritual well-being and quality of life among working-age cancer survivors.
gap_statement: Financial toxicity in cancer survivors is usually treated as a single construct, but the joint and separate effects of objective financial burden (OFB) and subjective financial distress (SFD) on spiritual well-being and HRQoL among working-age survivors in a universal-care setting are unclear.
framing_pattern: 'Objective vs subjective decomposition' framing: financial toxicity is decomposed into OFB (high medical payments, debt, bankruptcy) and SFD (perceived distress), and the four-way OFB x SFD cross-classification is used to dissect impacts on uncertainty, lost purpose, and HRQoL 'even in a universal care setting.'
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Multicenter cross-sectional survey of 727 working-age cancer survivors working at diagnosis (2017-2018) with measures of OFB, SFD, fear of cancer recurrence, spiritual well-being, and HRQoL.
methodology_choice.rationale: A cross-sectional 2x2 OFB/SFD design allows separation of objective and subjective financial constructs and their joint association with spiritual and HRQoL outcomes in a single working-age cohort.

key_finding: 31% of survivors experienced financial toxicity; the OFB-SFD group had 7.81-fold higher odds of uncertainty and 2.53-fold higher odds of lost purpose vs the No-OFB-No-SFD group, and overall QoL was lowest in OFB-SFD (41.2) and No-OFB-SFD (42.9), indicating SFD drove HRQoL decrement regardless of OFB; financial toxicity was tied to FCR, uncertainty, loss of purpose, and loss of hope even under universal care.
future_work: Inform survivors of the financial implications of cancer care so they can prepare financially as needed.

title: Risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma in breast cancer survivors: a nationwide cohort study.
gap_statement: Previous reports of an association between breast cancer and non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) were based on small NHL case numbers and lacked information on cancer treatment (especially anti-hormone therapy), menopausal status, and lifestyle variables that could confound second-malignancy risk.
framing_pattern: 'Suggestive but underpowered prior signals' framing: Japanese, Dutch, and Korean studies reported breast-cancer-to-NHL standardized incidence ratios ranging from 1.19 to 3.5 but 'had limitations because they provided little information about the type of cancer treatment, especially anti-hormone therapy,' so a nationwide cohort is needed.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean NHIS claims and health-screening data (2002-2016): 84,969 incident breast cancer cases vs 1,057,674 age- and region-matched controls, with breast cancer modeled as time-varying exposure and NHL subtype-specific outcomes (DLBCL, FL, mature T/NK-cell lymphomas, ALCL, others).
methodology_choice.rationale: A nationwide population cohort with linked screening data provides adequate NHL case numbers and lifestyle/menopause information to evaluate subtype- and treatment-specific risks that prior smaller studies could not.

key_finding: Breast cancer was associated with a 1.64-fold increased risk of NHL overall (95% CI 1.34-2.00), with much higher HRs in women <50 years (HR 2.91), in the first 2 years after diagnosis (HR 2.59), and among patients receiving anti-hormone therapy (HR 2.68); risks were particularly elevated for follicular lymphoma (HR 3.56), mature T/NK-cell lymphoma (HR 2.75), and ALCL (HR 7.46, remaining elevated >5 years).
limitations: Claims-based data lack laboratory, imaging, pathology, and reproductive details, and have no information on cancer stage, hormone type, or germline mutations; uncovered services (preventive surgery, OTC drugs, cosmetic surgery including breast implants) cannot be captured; potential surveillance bias.
future_work: Develop surveillance guidelines to screen for NHL in breast cancer patients, especially young patients and those receiving hormone therapy; further mechanistic studies of estrogen and androgen pathways in lymphomagenesis; future studies after sufficient follow-up of breast-reconstruction (implant) cohorts for BIA-ALCL.

title: Psychometric validation of the Korean Patient-Reported Outcome Measurement Information System (PROMIS)-29 Profile V2.1 in chronic pulmonary disease patients.
gap_statement: Commonly used PRO measures in chronic pulmonary disease (CAT, mMRC, SGRQ, EQ-5D, SF-36) either focus on respiratory symptoms or miss key extra-pulmonary symptoms such as fatigue, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbance, and the PROMIS-29 has not been validated for CPD patients in Korean.
framing_pattern: 'Each existing tool is partial' framing: CAT and mMRC miss extra-pulmonary symptoms, EQ-5D has large ceiling effects, SF-36 lacks fatigue/anxiety, so a more comprehensive generic profile measure (PROMIS-29) covering 'frequently reported symptoms such as fatigue and sleep disturbance in CPD patients' is needed, but it 'is not yet widely used among CPD patients.'
hypothesis: The Korean PROMIS-29 V2.1 will show acceptable reliability and construct validity in CPD patients, with high correlations between conceptually similar domains in K-PROMIS-29 and SF-36v2 and disease-specific measures (CAT, mMRC).

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional validation survey of 188 CPD outpatients (tuberculosis, NTM, bronchiectasis, COPD, lung cancer, ILD, asthma) at Samsung Medical Center (Sep-Oct 2018); K-PROMIS-29 V2.1, SF-36v2, CAT, and mMRC; EFA, CFA, internal consistency, convergent/discriminant validity.
methodology_choice.rationale: A standard psychometric pipeline (FACIT translation, EFA/CFA, convergent validity against SF-36 and disease-specific measures) was needed to establish that PROMIS-29 captures the full symptom profile of CPD patients in a Korean clinical population.

key_finding: K-PROMIS-29 V2.1 showed satisfactory internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha 0.77-0.95) and good CFA fit (CFI 0.90, SRMR 0.06); convergent validity was supported by strong correlations such as fatigue with SF-36 vitality (r=-0.75), physical function with SF-36 physical functioning (r=0.70), and mMRC with physical function (r=-0.80), although sleep-disturbance items split into 'sleep initiation' and 'sleep quality' constructs and anxiety/fatigue merged into one factor in EFA.
limitations: Single-institution Korean sample limits generalizability; no existing sleep-disturbance questionnaire was used to confirm convergent validity of the sleep subdomain.
future_work: Generate more data on how K-PROMIS-29 scores should be summarized and interpreted for CPD patients in clinical care; conduct additional cognitive interviews to clarify the anxiety/fatigue and sleep subdomain structure; promote routine use of K-PROMIS-29 in CPD research and clinical practice.

title: Efficacy of a tailored moisturizer for reducing chemotherapy-induced skin dryness in breast cancer patients: A randomized controlled clinical trial.
gap_statement: Chemotherapy-induced skin dryness is a common dermatologic adverse effect in breast cancer patients, but evidence from rigorous randomized trials on whether a tailored moisturizer reduces it is limited.
framing_pattern: Symptom-management trial framing: a targeted topical intervention (tailored moisturizer) is evaluated for a specific chemotherapy-induced dermatologic symptom in a defined cancer population.
hypothesis: A tailored moisturizer reduces chemotherapy-induced skin dryness in breast cancer patients compared with a control condition.

methodology_choice.data_source: Randomized controlled clinical trial in breast cancer patients receiving chemotherapy, comparing a tailored moisturizer with a control (full abstract not available).
methodology_choice.rationale: not stated

key_finding: not stated
future_work: not stated

title: The Relationship Between Breast Density Change During Menopause and the Risk of Breast Cancer in Korean Women.
gap_statement: While baseline mammographic breast density is a known breast cancer risk factor, the relationship between changes in breast density across the menopausal transition and subsequent breast cancer risk in Korean women has not been well established.
framing_pattern: 'Trajectory rather than single snapshot' framing: instead of a single baseline density measurement, the analysis tracks density change from premenopause to postmenopause to refine breast cancer risk stratification in Korean women.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Retrospective longitudinal cohort of women aged >30 who underwent serial screening mammography at baseline and after menopause at Samsung Medical Center; mammographic breast density classified by ACR BI-RADS; 18,615 person-years of follow-up (median 4.8 years).
methodology_choice.rationale: Repeated mammography during routine health checkups enabled longitudinal classification of density change without a separate research imaging protocol.

key_finding: Women whose breast density remained extremely dense from premenopause through postmenopause had a roughly two-fold higher cumulative incidence of breast cancer than other density-trajectory groups (375 vs 203 per 100,000 person-years, p<0.01), with cumulative incidence rising sharply 4 years after menopause.
future_work: Women with risk factors should receive mammography more frequently, and additional screening modalities should be considered when extremely dense breast tissue persists across menopause.

title: Effect of mind and body education on quality of life among young breast cancer patients: a randomized controlled trial.
gap_statement: Young breast cancer (YBC) patients face distinctive body-image, sexual, and emotional challenges, yet combined cosmetic-and-psychosocial ('mind and body') education programs have not been rigorously evaluated for this group.
framing_pattern: 'Combined-modality intervention for an underserved age subgroup' framing: appearance management and mind-control content are bundled into a structured 4-week program specifically for newly diagnosed young breast cancer patients.
hypothesis: A 4-week structured mind-and-body education program combining appearance management and mind control improves body image, sexual functioning, and emotional outcomes compared with usual care in young breast cancer patients.

methodology_choice.data_source: Unblinded RCT of 109 women newly diagnosed with stage I-III breast cancer (2014-2015) randomized to a 4-week mind-and-body education program (n=54) or control (n=55); outcomes assessed post-intervention and at 6 months (KCT0001191).
methodology_choice.rationale: An RCT design with 6-month follow-up was used to test both short-term and sustained efficacy of the combined educational intervention.

key_finding: The intervention group showed significantly better body image (75.0 vs 59.3, p<0.01), lower distress from altered appearance, and higher sexual functioning than controls, with effects maintained at 6 months post-intervention.
future_work: not stated

title: Validation of the Korean Version of the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System 29 Profile V2.1 among Cancer Patients
gap_statement: Generic profile HRQoL measures such as SF-36 and EQ-5D have documented limitations in cancer (missing cancer-relevant symptoms; not capturing all health-state aspects), while the newer PROMIS-29 V2.1 had only been validated in 'specific cancer patients' and had 'no psychometric evaluation' in a mixed cancer-survivor population, leaving Korean cancer patients without a validated generic profile measure.
framing_pattern: Tool-validation framing: contrasts cancer-specific (EORTC QLQ-C30, FACT-G) vs generic (SF-36, EQ-5D) measures, then positions PROMIS-29 V2.1 as filling the gap because 'comprehensive generic measures may add more predictive value for HRQoL of cancer patients than cancer-specific measures'.
hypothesis: The Korean version of PROMIS-29 V2.1 will demonstrate acceptable reliability, construct validity (via PCA and CFA), and convergent/discriminant validity with EORTC QLQ-C30 in Korean cancer patients.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional survey of 349 cancer outpatients at Samsung Medical Center Comprehensive Cancer Center (Sep-Oct 2018) completing K-PROMIS-29 V2.1 and EORTC QLQ-C30
methodology_choice.rationale: Cross-sectional psychometric design was chosen to evaluate internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha), structural validity (PCA, CFA), and convergent/discriminant validity against an established cancer-specific instrument.

key_finding: K-PROMIS-29 V2.1 showed satisfactory internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha 0.81-0.96), good model fit (CFI 0.91, SRMR 0.06, NNFI 0.90), and moderate-to-large convergent correlations with EORTC QLQ-C30 domains; however, depression and anxiety items loaded onto a single factor (r=0.80) and the sleep-disturbance domain split, suggesting the negative-affect overlap and the wording of 'refreshing' sleep need refinement.
limitations: Single-institution outpatient sample (generalizability), no test-retest reliability assessed, and no comparison with other generic measures such as SF-36 or EQ-5D.
future_work: Investigate hierarchical/multidimensional models of depression and anxiety to build more efficient computer adaptive tests, and conduct test-retest reliability studies in broader Korean cancer populations.

title: Author Correction: Perceived stress and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease in apparently healthy men and women
gap_statement: The original article (10.1038/s41598-019-57036-z) inadvertently switched the legends of Figures 1, 2, and 3, creating a mismatch between figure content and described content that needed formal correction.
framing_pattern: Erratum framing: brief technical notice that 'This Article contains an error in the Figure legends of Figure 1, Figure 2 and Figure 3. The legends of these Figures were inadvertently switched.'
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: Author correction notice referring to the original Scientific Reports article (PMID 31913326) on the Kangbuk Samsung Cohort Study
methodology_choice.rationale: Format is an author correction/erratum issued by the journal to fix a publication error; no new methods were applied.

key_finding: The figure legends for Figures 1-3 in the original NAFLD-stress paper were swapped; the corrected legends are provided so that Figure 1 shows the participant flowchart, Figure 2 shows the spline OR curve, and Figure 3 shows the subgroup OR comparison.
future_work: not stated

title: Association between body image dissatisfaction and poor quality of life and depression among patients with hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
gap_statement: Body image disturbance after hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is plausible given the visible appearance changes (skin, hair) and physical sequelae of conditioning regimens, yet the quantitative association between body image dissatisfaction and quality of life and depression in HSCT recipients had not been characterized.
framing_pattern: Survivorship framing applied beyond breast cancer to a hematologic-malignancy population: positions body image after HSCT as a measurable construct linked to mental health and HRQoL, transferring the breast-cancer altered-appearance lens to HSCT.
hypothesis: Body image dissatisfaction in HSCT patients is associated with poorer quality of life and higher prevalence of depressive symptoms.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional survey of 163 post-HSCT patients recruited from three university-based HSCT outpatient clinics and the Korea Blood Cancer Association, using Body Image Scale, WHOQOL-BREF, and PHQ-9
methodology_choice.rationale: A cross-sectional survey across multiple HSCT clinics and a patient advocacy group was chosen to capture a heterogeneous post-HSCT population and quantify associations via univariate and multivariate linear regression.

key_finding: Over 70% of HSCT patients felt less physically and sexually attractive, and 39.3% were dissatisfied with their body image; dissatisfied patients had significantly poorer quality of life (-13.68; 95% CI -18.16, -9.21) and were 8.59 times (95% CI 3.79, 19.48) more likely to have depressive symptoms.
future_work: Evaluate body image after HSCT routinely and develop appropriate interventions to prevent further psychological consequences.

title: Systematic Review of Studies Assessing the Health-Related Quality of Life of Hepatocellular Carcinoma Patients from 2009 to 2018
gap_statement: Prior systematic reviews of HRQoL in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) covered 1985-2013 and 'did not include recent studies, which reflect new HCC management strategies', and a 2001-2017 narrative review only addressed measurement tools rather than study design and HRQoL outcomes, leaving the modern era of RFA, TACE, and sorafenib unsynthesized.
framing_pattern: Synthesis/landscape framing: positions HRQoL as 'a strong predictor of survival in cancer patients' and motivates a review by enumerating dramatic 10-year changes in HCC prevention, diagnosis, and treatment, then declaring that 'this systematic review aims to evaluate studies assessing the HRQoL of HCC patients from 2009 to 2018'.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: PRISMA-guided systematic search of PubMed, Cochrane Library, PsycINFO, and Embase (2009-2018) yielding 45 included studies; risk of bias assessed with ROBINS-I
methodology_choice.rationale: A systematic review was chosen to map the heterogeneous, post-2009 HRQoL literature spanning observational and interventional designs; the heterogeneity of objectives and methods precluded a quantitative meta-analysis.

key_finding: HCC patients, including early-stage, had worse HRQoL than the general population, with fatigue, sleep disturbance, distress, sadness, and appetite loss persisting after treatment; TACE patients had worse long-term HRQoL than RFA or resection patients, poor HRQoL independently predicted all-cause mortality, yet only 4 of 45 studies tested interventions to improve HRQoL.
limitations: Restricted to HCC-only studies (excluded mixed liver-cancer cohorts), search limited to 'quality of life' terminology risking missed studies, and heterogeneity precluded quantitative synthesis.
future_work: More attention is needed to evaluate and improve HRQoL of HCC patients given rising HCC incidence and survivorship, particularly long-term survivors and supportive-care interventions, which remain understudied.

title: Perceived stress and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease in apparently healthy men and women
gap_statement: Although stress activates HPA/SNS pathways and drives unhealthy behaviors plausibly linked to NAFLD, evidence on this association is 'very limited' - prior work examined biological stress markers with liver disease or perceived stress with liver-related mortality, but 'no study has evaluated the association of perceived stress with NAFLD'.
framing_pattern: Risk-factor framing: builds a biological-plus-behavioral mechanism for stress (HPA axis, cortisol, catecholamines, plus smoking/alcohol/diet) and positions NAFLD as the missing endpoint in the stress-cardiometabolic literature, calling for identification of 'high-risk individuals before they develop NAFLD'.
hypothesis: Higher perceived stress, measured by the Perceived Stress Inventory (PSI), is independently associated with prevalent ultrasound-detected NAFLD in apparently healthy Korean adults.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional analysis of 171,321 participants in the Kangbuk Samsung Cohort Study (2011-2013) with PSI questionnaires and abdominal ultrasound
methodology_choice.rationale: Leveraging an existing large health-screening cohort with standardized PSI assessment and US-based NAFLD diagnosis allowed sufficient power for spline modeling and subgroup interaction tests with extensive covariate control.
