key_finding: PSI score was positively and nonlinearly associated with prevalent NAFLD after multivariable adjustment (top vs bottom quintile OR 1.17, 95% CI 1.11-1.22; 90th vs 10th percentile OR 1.16, 95% CI 1.11-1.20), with stronger associations in men (p-interaction <0.001) and obese participants (p-interaction 0.01).
limitations: Cross-sectional design (no causal inference), ultrasound rather than histology or MR spectroscopy for steatosis, self-reported single-measurement PSI without test-retest data, and a relatively young, healthy, educated Korean sample limiting generalizability.
future_work: Prospective studies and interventional trials testing stress management on metabolic abnormalities and NAFLD, and mechanistic studies on sex- and obesity-related differences in stress response.

title: Impact of a topical lotion, CG428, on permanent chemotherapy-induced alopecia in breast cancer survivors: a pilot randomized double-blind controlled clinical trial (VOLUME RCT)
gap_statement: Permanent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) is a common, distressing late effect in breast cancer survivors, yet most preventive or therapeutic strategies (scalp cooling, minoxidil, etc.) have shown limited efficacy or significant discomfort, leaving a need for tolerable topical interventions tested in a controlled trial.
framing_pattern: Intervention-development framing: moves from a 3-year observational PCIA cohort to a pilot RCT of a novel botanical lotion (citrus, cocoa, guarana, onion), positioning CG428 as a safe candidate worth testing for hair density/thickness recovery in survivors averaging 3.5 years post-chemotherapy.
hypothesis: Topical CG428 lotion applied twice daily for 6 months will improve hair density and thickness compared with placebo in breast cancer survivors with PCIA.

methodology_choice.data_source: Double-blind randomized controlled pilot trial of 35 breast cancer survivors with PCIA at Samsung Comprehensive Cancer Center (Feb-Dec 2016); hair density and thickness measured by noninvasive bioengineering device
methodology_choice.rationale: A pilot double-blind RCT was chosen to establish safety, tolerability, and preliminary efficacy signals of a novel botanical product before committing to a larger trial, using objective bioengineering measurements plus patient-reported outcomes.

key_finding: Hair density increased by 34.7% in the intervention group vs 24.9% in placebo (P=0.37) and hair thickness by 19.8% vs 35.6% (P=0.23); the trial showed safety and tolerability with a trend toward efficacy for hair density and self-reported improvement, though differences were not statistically significant in this small sample.
future_work: not stated

title: Permanent Chemotherapy-Induced Alopecia in Patients with Breast Cancer: A 3-Year Prospective Cohort Study
gap_statement: Although CIA is often treated as temporary, some patients have persistent alopecia years later, and 'there is, however, a paucity of long-term prospective data on the incidence and impact of permanent CIA (PCIA)', with prior studies limited by cross-sectional designs, patient-reported-only outcomes, single agents, lack of baseline hair data, or short follow-up.
framing_pattern: Methodologic-rigor framing: positions itself as 'the first prospective cohort study to evaluate the long-term incidence of PCIA' using both objective phototrichogram measurements (Folliscope) and patient-reported outcomes, anchored to pre-chemotherapy baseline.
hypothesis: not stated

methodology_choice.data_source: 61 stage I-III breast cancer patients receiving AC, FAC, or AC-docetaxel adjuvant chemotherapy at Samsung Medical Center (Feb 2012-Jul 2013), followed pre-chemo, after 2 cycles, at 1/3/6 months and 3 years post-chemo with objective hair density and thickness measurements
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective design with pre-chemo objective baseline and 3-year follow-up was needed to estimate true incidence of PCIA (defined as hair density/thickness >=2 SDs below baseline at >=6 months) and to test regimen-specific risk without recall bias.

key_finding: PCIA incidence was 39.5% at 6 months and 42.3% at 3 years; hair density recovered to baseline but hair thickness did not (52.7 vs 71.1 micrometers at 3 years); taxane-based regimens had ~8-fold higher PCIA odds (OR 8.01, 95% CI 1.20-53.26), and patients with PCIA had clinically meaningful worse body image scores (51.96 vs 70.19; P=0.014).
limitations: Single institution with small sample size limiting generalizability, hair parameters measured only on the vertex area, and potential self-selection bias toward participants interested in CIA or with prior hair problems.
future_work: Establish pathobiological mechanisms of PCIA, develop precise tools for subjective PCIA assessment, conduct larger studies on the effects of hormone therapy and different chemotherapy regimens, and design interdisciplinary supportive-care strategies (oncology, hair biology, dermatology, psychooncology).

title: Health-Related Quality of Life in Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma Survivors: A Prospective Cohort Study
gap_statement: NHL survivorship is growing, yet 'there are limited data comparing HRQOL between NHL diagnosis and survivorship', and 'previous studies did not compare HRQOL between aggressive and indolent NHL survivors', leaving uncertainty about whether the worse baseline HRQoL of aggressive NHL persists long-term.
framing_pattern: Diagnosis-to-survivorship-trajectory framing: leverages an existing prospective lymphoma cohort with pre-treatment HRQoL to compare aggressive vs indolent NHL longitudinally and to link baseline HRQoL with psychosocial, social, and spiritual well-being in survivors using QOL-CS.
hypothesis: HRQoL at diagnosis is associated with impaired psychosocial, social, and spiritual well-being during long-term NHL survivorship, and aggressive-NHL survivors may continue to have worse HRQoL than indolent-NHL survivors.

methodology_choice.data_source: Add-on study to the Samsung Medical Center Lymphoma Cohort Study (SMC-LCS): 370 NHL survivors (273 aggressive, 97 indolent) with baseline HRQoL (EORTC QLQ-C30) at diagnosis (2008-2011) and follow-up HRQoL plus QOL-CS at >=3 years (2014-2015)
methodology_choice.rationale: Re-contacting an existing prospective lymphoma cohort with baseline HRQoL allowed direct within-patient longitudinal comparison from diagnosis to survivorship, avoiding the recall and selection limitations of cross-sectional survivor studies.

key_finding: Long-term NHL survivors of aggressive (including stage III/IV) and indolent disease converged to similar HRQoL despite worse baseline scores in aggressive disease; however, >40% reported fear of relapse, impaired concentration/memory, insufficient support, and lack of life purpose, with baseline functional impairment and financial difficulties predicting these survivorship problems.
limitations: 22.7% non-response among consented survivors introducing selection bias toward better-HRQoL responders, reliance on self-reported outcomes, and limited covariate adjustment (only age, sex, diagnosis) due to small sample size.
future_work: Larger studies to identify risk factors in long-term NHL survivors, embed routine symptom and psychosocial monitoring in survivorship care, and test psychological interventions (MBSR, MBCT, ACT) to reduce fear of relapse.

title: Distress and body image due to altered appearance in posttreatment and active treatment of breast cancer patients and in general population controls
gap_statement: Long-term altered appearance and body image distress in posttreatment breast cancer patients had not been systematically compared with both patients in active treatment and an age- and area-matched general-population control group, leaving uncertain whether appearance-related distress resolves after treatment completion.
framing_pattern: Three-group comparative framing (posttreatment vs active treatment vs general population) of breast/hair/skin changes, distress, and body image, situating altered appearance as a long-term survivorship problem rather than an acute treatment side effect.
hypothesis: Posttreatment breast cancer patients will report lower altered-appearance levels and distress than active-treatment patients but still elevated levels compared with general-population controls.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional survey (May-Dec 2010) of 138 active-treatment and 128 posttreatment breast cancer patients from 23 Korean hospitals and 315 age- and area-matched general-population controls; measures included visual analogue scales and EORTC BR-23
methodology_choice.rationale: A multi-site cross-sectional design with matched general-population controls was chosen to benchmark appearance-related distress and body image against a non-cancer reference and to compare across treatment phases.

key_finding: Posttreatment patients reported breast, hair, and skin changes comparable to active-treatment patients and significantly worse than controls; for similar levels of altered appearance, breast cancer patients had higher distress than the general population, and high altered-appearance distress was associated with markedly poorer body image (-20.7, 95% CI -28.3 to -13.1).
future_work: Healthcare professionals should acknowledge long-term effects of altered appearance among breast cancer survivors and help them manage the associated distress and psychological consequences.

title: Who are happy survivors? Physical, psychosocial, and spiritual factors associated with happiness of breast cancer survivors during the transition from cancer patient to survivor
gap_statement: The reentry period for breast cancer survivors is marked by persistent QoL issues, yet the physical, psychosocial, and spiritual factors that explain why some survivors are nonetheless 'happy' had not been systematically identified, leaving clinicians without targets for happiness-oriented support.
framing_pattern: Positive-psychology framing within survivorship: flips the lens from deficits/distress to happiness ('Who are happy survivors?'), aligning HRQoL with subjective happiness and spiritual constructs (hope, purpose) during the diagnosis-to-survivor transition.
hypothesis: Physical, psychosocial, and spiritual factors (notably purpose and hope in life) are independently associated with happiness in breast cancer survivors during the reentry period.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional study of 283 nonmetastatic breast cancer survivors who completed treatment within 1 year and questionnaires (Subjective Happiness Scale, EORTC QLQ-C30) 2 years after diagnosis
methodology_choice.rationale: A cross-sectional survey at a defined survivorship timepoint (2 years post-diagnosis) allowed comparison of happy vs unhappy survivors using validated subjective happiness and QoL measures with multivariable logistic regression.

key_finding: 14.5% were 'very happy', 43.8% 'happy', 32.5% 'neutral', and 2.1% 'not happy at all'; happy survivors had better global health and QoL (67.6 vs 49.6; P<.01) and were independently more likely to report purpose (OR 2.50, 95% CI 1.42-4.40) and hope in life (OR 4.07, 95% CI 2.23-7.45).
future_work: Help breast cancer survivors set proper life goals as a potentially beneficial intervention for those experiencing persistent QoL issues during the reentry period.

title: Quantitative changes in skin composition parameters due to chemotherapy in breast cancer patients: a cohort study
gap_statement: Chemotherapy-induced skin changes in breast cancer patients were typically described subjectively, with little prospective quantitative data on objective skin composition (water, sebum, transepidermal water loss, melanin) before, during, and after chemotherapy, and on how these objective measures relate to patient-reported symptoms.
framing_pattern: Objectification framing: imports noninvasive bioengineering measurement of skin parameters into oncology survivorship to provide quantitative evidence underpinning patient-reported dermatologic complaints, paralleling the same group's objective-measurement approach to hair (Folliscope).
hypothesis: Adjuvant chemotherapy induces measurable changes in objective skin composition parameters (water content, sebum, TEWL, melanin) that persist beyond treatment and correlate with patient-reported dryness and dullness.

methodology_choice.data_source: Prospective cohort of 61 stage I-III postoperative breast cancer patients receiving adjuvant chemotherapy at a Korean outpatient breast cancer clinic (Feb-Sep 2012), with objective skin measurements via noninvasive bioengineering device at pre-chemo, after 2 cycles, and 1/3/6 months post-completion
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective design with serial objective bioengineering measurements anchored to a pre-chemotherapy baseline was needed to quantify within-patient skin changes over time and link them to subjective symptoms.

key_finding: Water content (-6.5%), sebum (-75.5%), and TEWL (-22.4%) significantly decreased during chemotherapy (all p<0.001) and did not return to baseline by 6 months; melanin increased during chemotherapy (+8.4%) then decreased afterward (-17.1%); most patients reported dryness (57.9%) and dullness (49.1%), with reported dryness significantly associated with decreased sebum.
future_work: Additional research is needed to translate these findings into interventions that improve the dermatologic quality of life of breast cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.
